coredata.dc:title: Preparation of high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots via solid-phase pyrolysis for highly sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescence probe detection of silver ions and glutathione
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Ma, Zixin
Yao, Zhuoru
Li, Peipei
Zhang, Jing
Zhang, Weijie
Zhao, Xiaoliang
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, folic acid-driven nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-FA-CQDs) were synthesized via solid-phase pyrolysis and utilized as a highly sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescence probe for the detection of silver ions (Ag+) and glutathione (GSH). The N-FA-CQDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of up to 48.51 % and demonstrate excellent optical stability. The detection mechanism is based on the fluorescence of N-FA-CQDs being quenched by Ag+ through an aggregation-caused quenching mechanism, followed by recovery of fluorescence via competitive binding of GSH with Ag+. The detection ranges for Ag+ and GSH are 0–70 μM and 0–140 μM, respectively, with detection limits of 1.07 μM and 0.15 μM, respectively. Importantly, satisfactory recovery rates were achieved in the spiked recovery tests of real samples, indicating the potential practical application value of the prepared fluorescence probe. In summary, the “on-off-on” detection method based on N-FA-CQDs exhibits dual functionality and higher sensitivity and selectivity compared to traditional “on-off” probes, making it a promising new approach for the fluorescence analysis of Ag+ and GSH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid phase pyrolysis method
Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
“on-off-on” fluorescent probe
Silver ion
Glutathione

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 3D fluorescence of N-FA-CQDs, (b) absorption and 2D fluorescence spectra of N-FA-CQDs. (c) Fluorescence spectra under different excitation. (d) Normalized fluorescence spectra.
(a) PL intensity of N-FA-CQDs in different pH solutions. (b) Linear relationship between F/F0 and pH. (c) Photobleaching resistance and (d) ion concentration resistance of N-FA-CQDs.
(a) TEM image of N-FA-CQDs, with the inset showing the particle size distribution histogram. (b) HRTEM image, (c) XRD pattern and (d) FT-IR spectrum of N-FA-CQDs.
(a) XPS full spectra of N-FA-CQDs. XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s.

(a) Selectivity of N-FA-CQDs towards Ag+ and N-FA-CQDs+Ag+ towards GSH, (b, c) Fluorescence spectra and linear relationship of N-FA-CQDs in the presence of Ag+ at different concentrations, (d, e) Fluorescence spectra and linear relationship of N-FA-CQDs+Ag+ for detecting GSH.

(a) TEM image of N-FA-CQDs in the presence of Ag+. XPS spectra of (b) the N 1s and (c) the O 1s regions for N-FA-CQDs and N-FA-CQDs+Ag+. (d) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of N-FA-CQDs, N-FA-CQDs + Ag+, and N-FA-CQDs + Ag+ + GSH.
Comparison of proposed probe with other methods for the detection of GSH.
The spiked recovery results of Ag+ and GSH in real samples.

head.ce:title: Preparation of high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots via solid-phase pyrolysis for highly sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescence probe detection of silver ions and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Health Engineering, Lanzhou University of Information Science and Technology, Lanzhou 730050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Health Engineering, Lanzhou University of Information Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Health Engineering, Lanzhou University of Information Science and Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, folic acid-driven nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-FA-CQDs) were synthesized via solid-phase pyrolysis and utilized as a highly sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescence probe for the detection of silver ions (Ag+) and glutathione (GSH). The N-FA-CQDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of up to 48.51 % and demonstrate excellent optical stability. The detection mechanism is based on the fluorescence of N-FA-CQDs being quenched by Ag+ through an aggregation-caused quenching mechanism, followed by recovery of fluorescence via competitive binding of GSH with Ag+. The detection ranges for Ag+ and GSH are 0–70 μM and 0–140 μM, respectively, with detection limits of 1.07 μM and 0.15 μM, respectively. Importantly, satisfactory recovery rates were achieved in the spiked recovery tests of real samples, indicating the potential practical application value of the prepared fluorescence probe. In summary, the “on-off-on” detection method based on N-FA-CQDs exhibits dual functionality and higher sensitivity and selectivity compared to traditional “on-off” probes, making it a promising new approach for the fluorescence analysis of Ag+ and GSH.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), a crucial non-protein thiol in the body, plays a significant role in cellular antioxidant defense systems, gene regulation, heterologous metabolism, and intracellular signal transduction [1–3]. Changes in GSH concentrations have been linked to various health conditions including cancer, heart disease, neurodegenerative diseases, leukopenia, diabetes, and AIDS [4–6]. Therefore, the measurement of GSH levels in the body holds immense importance in the early diagnosis and treatment of these diseases. Current analytical techniques utilized for detecting GSH levels in the body encompass high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical methods, enhanced Raman scattering, mass spectrometry, magnetic resonance imaging, and colorimetric methods. While these techniques are effective, their widespread application is hindered by their time-consuming nature, complex operation, and the requirement of expensive instruments [7–9]. Consequently, there is a pressing need to develop a straightforward, rapid, precise, and cost-effective analytical method for GSH detection. Silver, as a precious metal, has extensive applications in daily life. However, the misuse of silver salts can lead to environmental pollution and health risks [10]. Therefore, the determination of silver ion (Ag+) concentration in water is of critical importance. Traditional methods for detecting Ag+ include inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [11], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12], and atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [13]. While these methods offer high precision and reliability, their high instrumental costs and complex operational procedures limit their widespread application. Consequently, the development of a convenient, rapid, and cost-effective method for Ag+ detection is highly significant.

Fluorescent probes based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely utilized in the detection of biomolecules, such as glucose [14], cholesterol [15], and ascorbic acid [16], as well as metal ions like Fe3+ [17] and Cu2+ [18], and in bioimaging applications [19] due to their ease of use, excellent biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity [20]. Recent studies have also reported on the use of CQDs-based fluorescent probes for the detection of Ag+ and GSH. For example, Hou et al. utilized catechol and hydrazine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, to synthesize CQDs with a quantum yield of 28.2 % via a simple hydrothermal method. These CQDs were employed as an “on-off” type fluorescence probe for the detection of Ag+, achieving a detection limit of 1.1 µM. Satisfactory results were obtained in practical sample analysis [21]. Kamal et al. synthesized nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CQDs with a quantum yield of up to 63 % using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as the carbon and nitrogen precursor, and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as the nitrogen and phosphorus dopant, via a one-step microwave heating method. These quantum dots were utilized as an “on-off” type fluorescent probe for the detection of GSH, achieving a detection limit of 0.16 μg/mL [22]. While the aforementioned studies have made significant progress, regrettably, they are unable to simultaneously detect Ag+ and GSH. If a fluorescent probe could be developed to simultaneously detect both Ag+ and GSH, it would hold significant practical value and represent an intriguing area of research. Fortunately, similar studies have been reported in the literature. For instance, Sun et al. [23] and Mahmoud et al.[24] have developed “on-off-on” fluorescent probes based on CQDs for the simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and GSH. However, there are few reports on CQDs-based fluorescent probes for the simultaneous detection of Ag+ and GSH. Fortunately, the success of the aforementioned studies provides valuable impetus for the development of such probes capable of detecting both Ag+ and GSH.

Currently, one of the key obstacles limiting the development of highly sensitive CQDs-based fluorescent probes is the low quantum yield, which significantly diminishes the sensitivity of the probes. Heteroatom doping is a crucial technique for modulating the physical and chemical properties of quantum dots and enhancing their quantum yield [25]. Among various heteroatoms, nitrogen, due to its similar atomic radius to carbon and its unique lone pair of electrons, has the potential to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield by modifying the electronic structure and surface state of CQDs [26–28]. The fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) is primarily conducted through hydrothermal or solvent thermal methods, which, though effective, pose potential risks due to the elevated temperature and pressure within sealed vessels. Hence, it is imperative to devise a preparation approach that ensures safety and eliminates the hazards associated with high temperature and pressure for the production of N-CQDs. In our previous research, we introduced solid-phase pyrolysis methods carried out in an open environment, which offer a safe, efficient, and convenient alternative with high product quantum yields [29]. This innovative method indeed has great potential to produce N-CQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield, and it is also safe, which could be a reliable choice for producing high-quality N-CQDs in the future.

Inspired by the aforementioned findings, a rapid and cost-effective strategy for synthesizing N-FA-CQDs (Folic acid-driven nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots) through solid-phase pyrolysis in an open environment using FA and urea has been proposed. The resulting N-FA-CQDs exhibit high quantum yields. The optical properties and structural composition of N-FA-CQDs were characterized using TEM, XRD, XPS, UV-Vis, and PL analyses. Subsequently, N-FA-CQDs were integrated with silver as a quencher to develop an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Ag+ and GSH, yielding excellent detection outcomes. Additionally, the practical application potential of this fluorescent probe was evaluated through recovery experiments involving Ag+ and GSH spiking in real samples.

FA was supplied from Shanghai Ika Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Urea was purchased from Tianjin Jindong Tianzheng fine chemical reagent factory. GSH was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Shanghai Co. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (AR) was acquired from PAI NI Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou, China). Sulfuric acid was got from Xi'an Sanpu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Reagents for interference detection were placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information section. All the reagents were analytical grade and had not undergone further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment process.
The instruments information of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

The synthesis of N-FA-CQDs was carried out via solid-phase pyrolysis under ambient conditions. Initially, a mixture of 0.05 g folic acid (FA) and 0.3 g urea was thoroughly ground and homogenized in a mortar. The resulting blend was transferred to a crucible and subjected to a thermal reaction at 180 ℃ for 2.0 h. Post-reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. Thereafter, 500 mL of ultrapure water was introduced, followed by 10 min of ultrasonication to ensure maximal dissolution of the product. The solution was subsequently filtered, centrifuged, and dialyzed to remove both large and small molecular impurities, thereby yielding a purified aqueous solution of N-FA-CQDs. This solution was then dried in an oven at 60 ℃ to obtain a light yellow powder, which was stored at a low temperature for future use.

The QY of N-FA-CQDs were tested and calculated using the previously reported method [30]. The calculation was performed by the following equation using quinine sulfate as a reference.
(1)
φ
x
=
φ
s
×
Grad
x
Grad
s
×
η
x
2
η
s
2
.
(2)
Grad =
I
A

Quinine sulfate was dissolved in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, demonstrating a quantum yield (QY) of 54 %. Within the scope of this investigation, the parameters I, η, φ, and A are defined as the integral area of fluorescence intensity, the refractive index of the solvent, the quantum yield, and the UV–vis absorbance, respectively. The slope, designated as Grad, was calculated from the linear correlation between the integrated fluorescence intensity and the absorbance. In this context, 'x' signifies the sample under examination, whereas 's' represents the reference standard.

To a 5 mL tube, 4.5 mL of the N-FA-CQDs solution was added. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of AgNO3 solutions with different concentrations were added to the tubes to achieve a final Ag⁺ concentration range of 0–70 μM. The mixtures were vigorously shaken and incubated for an additional 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra of these solutions were subsequently measured and recorded for analysis.

GSH does not directly interact with N-FA-CQDs to modulate fluorescence intensity. Rather, the fluorescence of N-FA-CQDs was initially quenched through the introduction of Ag+ as a quenching agent. Subsequently, the fluorescence of N-FA-CQDs was reinvigorated by the addition of GSH, which formed complexes with Ag+. This methodology facilitated the detection of GSH as elucidated below.

To a 5 mL tube, 4 mL of the N-FA-CQDs solution was added. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of AgNO3 solution with a concentration of 70 μM was introduced, followed by thorough shaking and incubation for 10 min at room temperature. Different concentrations of GSH solutions (0.5 mL) were then added to the tubes to achieve a final GSH concentration range of 0–140 μM. The mixtures were vigorously shaken and incubated for an additional 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra of these solutions were subsequently measured and recorded for analysis.

To achieve N-FA-CQDs with optimal optical properties, the influence of the nitrogen source addition amount, reaction temperature, and reaction time was systematically optimized. The fluorescence spectra of N-FA-CQDs synthesized using 0.05 g of FA with varying masses of urea are presented in Fig. S1a. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a gradual increase to a maximum value as the urea amount was increased from 0 g to 0.3 g. Beyond this point, further increases in urea resulted in a decline in fluorescence intensity, thereby identifying 0.3 g of urea as the optimal amount. Under this material ratio, the reaction temperature and time were subsequently optimized to ensure the preparation of N-FA-CQDs with the best optical properties. As depicted in Fig. S1b and Fig. S1c, the fluorescence intensity of N-FA-CQDs prepared at excessively low temperatures and short reaction times was comparatively low. This reduction in intensity is primarily attributed to incomplete reaction of the raw materials under these conditions. Consequently, the optimal reaction temperature and time were determined to be 180 ℃ and 2.0 h, respectively, to ensure comprehensive reaction and enhanced fluorescence properties. Under these optimal conditions, the 3D fluorescence profile of N-FA-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1a, revealing the optimal emission center at (EX=360 nm, EM=445 nm). The corresponding 2D fluorescence spectrum is presented in Fig. 1b, showcasing an optimal excitation wavelength at 360 nm and an optimal emission wavelength at 445 nm. Notably, under UV illumination, the N-FA-CQDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence, as evidenced by the inset in Fig. 1b. The UV absorption spectrum of N-FA-CQDs displays a peak at 280 nm, which corresponds to the π-π* transition of CC bonds [31]. Additionally, an absorption peak at 360 nm is observed, attributed to the n-π* transition of CO and CN groups [32]. The change of fluorescence emission spectrum with excitation wavelength was tested. As shown in Fig. 1c, a significant variation in the emission spectrum intensity was observed when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 400 nm. To determine whether the position of the emission peak had shifted, the emission spectra were normalized, as illustrated in Fig. 1d. It can be seen that when the excitation center is at 300 nm, the emission center is located at 353 nm. However, as the excitation center gradually shifts from 320 nm to 400 nm, the emission center experiences a 10 nm red shift, moving from 444 nm to 454 nm. By combining the analysis of the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra and the emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths, we conclude that the prepared N-FA-CQDs exhibit a pronounced excitation-dependent between 300 nm and 320 nm, while showing a slight excitation-dependent between 320 nm and 400 nm. Furthermore, the quantum yield (QY) of N-FA-CQDs was determined to be 48.51 %, utilizing quinine sulfate as a reference standard (Fig. S2). This value is notably high compared to those reported in existing literature, underscoring the exceptional fluorescence properties of N-FA-CQDs synthesized under these optimized conditions. The mechanisms responsible for the high quantum yield induced by nitrogen doping have been reported to include the introduction of new energy levels, passivation of surface states, and optimization of electronic properties [26–28,33]. However, the specific mechanisms underlying these improvements require further investigation in future studies.

The stability of N-FA-CQDs was systematically evaluated through pH-dependent, photobleaching resistance, and ion concentration resistance experiments. As shown in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence intensity of N-FA-CQDs was measured over a wide pH range from 1 to 14. The experimental results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of N-FA-CQDs exhibits significant pH dependence in acidic conditions (pH 1–6), while it remains relatively stable under neutral to alkaline conditions (pH 7–14). The pronounced changes in fluorescence intensity under acidic conditions may be attributed to the extensive protonation/deprotonation of surface amide and/or carboxyl groups [34,35]. When the pH decreases from 6 to 1, the fluorescence intensity gradually diminishes. As demonstrated in Fig. 2b, the normalized fluorescence intensity (F/F0) shows a robust linear correlation with pH values in the range of 1–6 (R = 0.983). This linear relationship suggests the potential utility of N-FA-CQDs as a fluorescent probe for pH detection within this range. However, this study primarily focused on evaluating the stability of N-FA-CQDs rather than their application in pH sensing. The photobleaching resistance of N-FA-CQDs was assessed by subjecting the samples to continuous UV irradiation for 2 h. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the fluorescence intensity remained largely unchanged, indicating excellent photostability and highlighting the probe's potential for applications requiring prolonged UV exposure. In the ion concentration resistance experiments, as shown in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence intensity remained stable even in the presence of 1 mol/L NaCl solution, demonstrating minimal sensitivity to ionic strength. These results collectively indicate that the prepared N-FA-CQDs exhibit strong stability under non-acidic conditions and are well-suited for applications across neutral to alkaline environments.

The morphology, crystal structure, and composition of N-FA-CQDs were characterized using TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS. Fig. 3a presents the TEM image of N-FA-CQDs, indicating good dispersion and a nearly spherical shape. The particle size distribution ranges from 1.15 to 2.91 nm, with an average particle size of 2.04 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 3b) reveals well-defined lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of the graphite phase [36]. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 3c) shows a broad diffraction peak at approximately 22.3°, which is consistent with the characteristic (002) diffraction peak of the graphite phase [37]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3d) indicates the presence of various functional groups, including -NH₂, -OH, and -COOH [38–40]. XPS analysis further elucidates the elemental composition and bonding types of N-FA-CQDs. Fig. 4a shows that N-FA-CQDs primarily consist of C, N, and O elements with atomic ratios of 69.5 %, 17.6 %, and 12.9 %, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) is deconvoluted into peaks at 284.76 eV (CC/C-C), 286.16 eV (C-N/C-O), and 288.74 eV (CN/CO) [41]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) is deconvoluted into peaks at 399.81 eV (C-N = C) and 400.83 eV (-NH₂) [42]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) is deconvoluted into peaks at 531.88 eV (CO) and 533.47 eV (O-CO) [43]. The FT-IR and XPS results collectively demonstrate that N-FA-CQDs possess a variety of hydrophilic functional groups, which enhance their water solubility and facilitate their application in aqueous environments.

The fluorescence of N-FA-CQDs is not directly influenced by GSH, rendering them unsuitable for direct GSH detection. However, GSH exhibits a robust capacity for chelating metal ions, which enables it to complex with quenching metal ions, thereby restoring the fluorescence of the probe. This principle forms the basis for GSH detection. In this study, Ag+ was selected as the metal ion quenching agent, and the selectivity of N-FA-CQDs towards Ag+ was initially assessed. As depicted in Fig. 5a (green column), among various interferents, only Ag+ significantly quenched the fluorescence of N-FA-CQDs, while other interferents had negligible effects on fluorescence intensity. This indicates that N-FA-CQDs exhibit excellent selectivity for the quenching agent Ag+. Subsequently, the selectivity of the N-FA-CQDs+Ag+ system towards GSH was examined (red column in Fig. 5a). The results revealed that only GSH could significantly restore the fluorescence quenched by Ag+, confirming the high selectivity of the N-FA-CQDs+Ag+ fluorescent probe system for GSH. The impact of quencher addition on the fluorescence intensity of N-FA-CQDs was then investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c. The fluorescence intensity of N-FA-CQDs exhibited a linear decrease with increasing Ag+ concentrations from 0 to 70 μM, described by the linear relationship equation F/F0 = 1.035–0.014 C[Ag+] with an R² value of 0.986 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.07 μM (where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity before and after Ag+ addition, respectively). Employing 70 μM of Ag+ as the quenching agent, it was added to the N-FA-CQDs solution for GSH detection, as shown in Fig. 5d and Fig. 5e. The fluorescence of the system linearly recovered with increasing GSH concentrations from 0 to 140 μM, governed by the equation F/F0 = 0.223 + 0.100 C[GSH] with an R² of 0.985 and a LOD of 0.15 μM, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity before and after GSH addition in the N-FA-CQDs+ 70 μM Ag+ system, respectively. The detection mechanism can be elucidated as follows: Ag+ induces aggregation-induced quenching of N-FA-CQDs by binding to -NH2 and -OH groups on the surface of N-FA-CQDs. In contrast, the -SH groups on GSH have a strong affinity for Ag+ and chelate with Ag+, thereby redispersing the previously aggregated CQDs and restoring fluorescence. The hypothesis that Ag+ induce the aggregation of N-FA-CQDs was verified through TEM characterization. As shown in Fig. 6a, the addition of Ag+ led to significant aggregation of N-FA-CQDs, confirming the mechanism of aggregation-caused quenching. Furthermore, the binding mode between Ag+ and N-FA-CQDs was investigated using XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 6b and Fig. 6c, the content of -NH2 and O-CO in N-FA-CQDs + Ag+ is reduced compared to that in N-FA-CQDs, indicating that Ag+ binds to -NH2 and -OH. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence lifetime of the detection system decreased from 6.404 ns to 5.966 ns upon the addition of Ag+, indicating that Ag+ affects the electron transition of N-FA-CQDs, which is characteristic of dynamic quenching. In contrast, aggregation-caused quenching is typically associated with static quenching [44]. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Ag+ on N-FA-CQDs fluorescence can be summarized as a synergistic quenching mechanism involving both static and dynamic quenching. Subsequently, it can be observed that the fluorescence lifetime recovered to 6.314 ns after the addition of GSH, nearly equal to that of the N-FA-CQDs without Ag+ addition. This confirms that GSH effectively displaces the Ag+ bound to the surface of N-FA-CQDs, thereby restoring the fluorescence.

To evaluate the detection performance of the proposed fluorescent probe for GSH, it was compared with previously reported GSH detection methods. As shown in Table 1, this work presents fluorescence probes with a wider detection range and lower detection limits, resulting in superior detection performance. Furthermore, the probe substrate used in this method is simple, environmentally friendly, and exhibits excellent optical performance, making it a promising candidate for practical disease diagnosis and detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ and GSH in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical performance of the fluorescent probe, the prepared N-FA-CQDs fluorescent probe was used to conduct spike tests for Ag+ in tap water and GSH in human urine samples. The tap water samples were obtained from the laboratory tap water source and were subjected to a simple pre-filtration using a 0.22 μm microporous filter before the spike tests. The spiked concentrations were 15 μM, 40 μM, and 60 μM. The test results are presented in Table 2, with the obtained recovery rates ranging from 94.576 % to 102.045 %. The urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers in the laboratory. Sample processing followed the procedures described in the literature [47]. Specifically, to minimize the fluorescence background from unaltered urine samples, the collected specimens were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate particulate contaminants. The resulting supernatant was then collected and diluted 100-fold with PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4). Subsequently, the GSH detection procedure described in Section 2.6 was followed to analyze the GSH content in the urine samples using the spike method. The spiked concentrations were 15 μM, 25 μM, and 45 μM. The test results are presented in Table 2, with the obtained recovery rates ranging from 94.281 % to 105.164 %. These results demonstrate the potential feasibility of the developed “on-off-on” N-FA-CQDs fluorescent probe for detecting Ag+ and GSH in real samples.

In conclusion, blue fluorescent N-FA-CQDs with an exceptionally high quantum yield (48.51 %) were prepared via solid-phase pyrolysis. These N-FA-CQDs exhibited a uniform particle size distribution with an average size of 2.04 nm and clear lattice fringes at a spacing of 0.20 nm. The surface of N-FA-CQDs was rich in hydrophilic groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which endowed them with excellent water solubility and strong affinity for Ag+. Fluorescence quenching occurs due to aggregation when Ag+ interact with the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the N-FA-CQDs. However, the thiol groups in GSH possess a stronger binding affinity for Ag+, allowing GSH to effectively displace Ag+ from the N-FA-CQDs and restore the fluorescence of the system. Based on this mechanism, we constructed an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the detection of Ag+ and GSH. The detection range for Ag+ was 0–70 μM, with a LOD of 1.07 μM. For GSH, the detection range was 0–140 μM, and the LOD was 0.15 μM. Importantly, the probe exhibited excellent recovery rates (94.281–105.164 %) in spike recovery experiments with real samples, indicating its potential for practical applications. Compared to other reported sensors, this “on-off-on” fluorescent probe demonstrates dual functionality, offering a novel detection technology with potential practical applications for Ag+ and GSH.

Fan Jun: Funding acquisition. Zhang Weijie: Funding acquisition. Zhao Xiaoliang: Funding acquisition. Li Peipei: Investigation. Zhang Jing: Investigation. Ma Zixin: Investigation. Yao Zhuoru: Investigation. Wang Cunjin: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 32260231), the Gansu Provincial Natural Science Fund (No. 23JRRA840), the PhD Start-up Foundation of Lanzhou University of Technology, and Red Willow Excellent Youth Talent Support Program of Lanzhou University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136416.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe (CQDs-O-NBD) based on carbon quantum dots for detection and discrimination of Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S in living cells
Dyes Pigments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, H. Jia, W. Liu, J. Wang, D. Fang, A novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe (CQDs-O-NBD) based on carbon quantum dots for detection and discrimination of Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S in living cells, Dyes. Pigments, 193 (2021) 109554.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A cellulosic multi-bands fluorescence probe for rapid detection of pH and glutathione
